observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and ICO – Municipal Accountant to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds, limit the expenditures within the total income realized during the year and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated, in compliance with Section 2 of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Section 337 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality held a series of meetings with the finance committee to increase target income and reduce expenditures without compromising the services that were provided to constituents.
observations.reason: Cash overdraft still existed at year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the ICO – Municipal Accountant to strengthen the monitoring of cash advances and stop the practice of granting additional CA unless the previous CAs granted were liquidated in compliance with Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by the Management.
observations.reason: The four disbursing officers were still granted multiple CAs during the year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure
that the assessed values of real properties provided by the Municipal Assessor’s
Office is strictly used in the determination of tax collectibles during the year.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer,
Municipal Assessor and Municipal Accountant to implement measures to come up
with integrated and reconciled records which will result in an accurate and reliable
set-up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Schedule of Unreleased Checks monthly and
forward the same to the Municipal Accountant, in compliance with Section 2.3
of COA-GAFMIS Circular Letter No. 2002-001; and
b. Municipal Accountant to prepare a JEV at year-end to record the restoration of
unreleased checks to the Cash in Bank – LCCA and recognition of the
appropriate liability account to reflect its correct balances in the financial
statements, in compliance with Section 2.4 of the same Circular Letter.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
concerned officers to:

a. Submit written justification on the use of CA and/or reimbursement as mode of
payment for the noted transactions;

b. Discontinue the practice of procuring goods and services that exceed the
P1,000.00 threshold through CA and/or reimbursement basis, instead follow the
regular procurement process, directly pay the expenses by check to the
supplier/service provider, and withhold and remit the taxes provided under
relevant tax laws and regulation issued by the BIR; and

c. Consider setting-up petty cash fund for payments of small transactions where it may
be very difficult, impractical or impossible to make payments through checks; and

d. Review past transactions and implement proper planning and scheduling of
programs, projects and activities (PPAs) to minimize reliance on cash advances and minimize
occurrence of reimbursement to emergency cases only.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to inform/remind the concerned contractors/suppliers of
their outstanding claims against the Municipality and thereafter release the
amounts due to them;
b. Municipal Accountant to expedite the review/verification of recorded
transactions and pertinent records to identify the cause of the noted variance
and prepare the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to come up
with the reconciled balance; and
c. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that retention money
is released to the concerned contractor/supplier upon the lapse of the warranty
period of the implemented project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, in
coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson, to
immediately submit the noted certifications from FDA and manufacturers to the
Office of the Auditor to avoid suspension of the transactions.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Chairperson to
ensure that participating suppliers of drugs and medicines are equipped with the
necessary certifications required by Section 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Refrain from granting additional/new CA to accountable officers with
unliquidated CAs; and

b. Ensure that the previous CA is liquidated before granting of a new one
evidenced by a Certification that the previous CA has been fully liquidated and
accounted for in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, in
coordination with the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Human Resource
Management Officer, to implement stringent measures in the payroll preparation to
ensure that the approval of loan applications of personnel should not cause their
net take home pay to be lower than the mandatory limit of P5,000.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Officer to:

a. Submit the lacking supporting documents of the GAD AR;
b. Propose a training program on the HGDG tool in the GPB;
c. Conduct gender analysis through the administration of the HGDG tool to
increase the gender-responsiveness of the Municipality’s PPAs; and
d. Ensure complete submission of GAD AR with the required attachments as
prescribed by PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 to facilitate the
thorough evaluation and assessment of GAD PPAs undertaken by the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Human Resources
Management Officer and Municipal Treasurer to expedite the adoption of the ATM
payroll system for all Municipal personnel to safeguard government funds from risk
of loss and/or misappropriation associated with the manual payroll system.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant (i) prepare and submit to the AT the SUCA using the prescribed format, ensuring completeness for proper validation and tracking; (ii) maintain the required general and subsidiary ledgers, as well as subsidiary schedules, in compliance with Sections 111(1) and 114(2) of PD No. 1445; (iii) send regular written notices to all concerned AOs to demand immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances; and (iv) enforce strict internal control on the granting of cash advances to ensure proper settlement of previous ones before granting any additional cash advances; and
b. Municipal Mayor (i) consider suspending/withholding of salaries of AOs with outstanding balances who are still connected with the Municipality, subject to the minimum take-home pay requirement under the General Appropriations Act; (ii) initiate appropriate legal action against AOs who deliberately failed to liquidate their cash advances after due notice, if warranted; and (iii) ensure full compliance by the Municipality with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the members of the Inventory Committee to promptly conduct physical count of inventories and prepare the corresponding RPCI in compliance with existing rules;
b. Facilitate the reconciliation of the results of the physical count with the accounting records to enable the recording of necessary adjustments and ensure the accurate valuation of inventories in the books of accounts; and
c. Institute measures to ensure the timely conduct of annual physical inventory and submission of the corresponding report.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough review and reconciliation of the “Due to GSIS,” “Due to Pag-IBIG,” and “Due to PhilHealth” accounts to identify the root causes of the negative balances observed during CY 2024. This should include (i) verifying the completeness and accuracy of recorded contributions and (ii) ensuring proper timing of remittance and withholding entries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to provide the ICO – Municipal Accountant with the certified list showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and collectible as the basis of the latter in setting-up Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the ICO – Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to fast track reconciliation of the deficiency noted between the balance of the RPT and SET Receivables and the Certified List of Delinquent Taxes as of December 31, 2023 and make the necessary adjustments in their records, if warranted.
Moving forward, instruct the Municipal Treasurer and ICO – Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and Section 200 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer has already provided the ICO – Municipal Accountant with the certified list showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and collectible.
Reconciliation of records has already been made.

observations.managementAction: Proper monitoring and continuous checking of the Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms is now being observed by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate timely submission of the DVs and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor in accordance with COA Circular No. 95-006 as reiterated in Section 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor directs the ICO – Municipal Accounting Office and the ICO – Municipal Treasurer to: a. conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and determine the concerned debtors according to paragraph 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005; b. exert effort in determining the collectability of the receivables, issue demand letters, and institute all possible remedies to all concerned individuals found on their last known address with outstanding balances; c. set up an allowance for impairment for recognition of possible losses of P229,600.00 according to paragraphs 67 and 72 of IPSAS 29; and d. consider requesting authority to write off receivables with no certainty of collection from the COA, with the reminder for the submission of all proofs that efforts have been initiated to collect the same. In addition, require the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer to provide all possible records for the receivable balance of P78,200.00 relative to the defaulting debtor and coordinate with the ICO – Municipal Accountant and the ICO – Municipal Treasurer for possible settlement of the long outstanding receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Management validated and analyzed and verified the existence of the receivables. JEV no. 300-2023-11-043 was drawn by the ICO – Municipal Accountant to set up an allowance for impairment. There is an on-going preparation of documents to request for write-off of the receivables. Request for write-off with attached death certificate and certificate of indigency were submitted. JEV no. 300-2023-11-043 was drawn by the ICO – Municipal Accountant to correct the erroneous entry made relative to the receivable amounting to P78,200.00.
observations.reason: There is an on-going preparation of documents to request for write-off of the receivables.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the OIC - Municipal Accounting Office to: a. evaluate the validity and propriety of some dormant payables, which has been already outstanding for more than two years, to determine/confirm whether there are actual claimants and these dormant payables are supported with proper documentation; and b. seek authority from the Sanggunian Bayan for the reversion of dormant payables, except unliquidated balances of Accounts Payable in the Trust Fund, to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund pursuant to Section 98 PD No. 1445; and for unutilized portion of already completed projects/programs, return the same to the source agency.
observations.managementAction: The Management evaluated the validity and propriety of the claims. A reversion of dormant payables was made.

ICO – Municipal Treasurer to provide the ICO – Municipal Accountant with the certified list showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and collectible as basis of the latter in setting-up Real Property Tax Receivables and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year for CY 2020; and b. Inability to conduct implemented regular and periodic verification of the receivables.
Reiterated in AOM No. 2022-012 (2021)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal GAD Focal Point Chairperson to review the unimplemented GAD PPAs if these are still feasible and ensure the implementation of the same to maximize the utilization of GAD funds and attain the objective for which funds were provided.
observations.managementAction:
No action was taken by the Management.
Reiterated in AOM No. 2022-005 (2021).
observations.reason: Due to COVID-19 restrictions, the management fail to maximize the utilization of GAD funds and attain the objective for which funds were provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Accountant: Send demand letters to the former elected officials and those separated from the service by exerting effort in locating their present addresses and ensuring they receive the same to enforce immediate liquidation/settlement of their cash advances; Ensure strict compliance with the rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances as provided under COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and Section 89 of PD No. 1445 to prevent the accumulation of outstanding balances of cash advances; and Lastly, exhaust available remedies as provided under COA Circular No. 2012-004 to enforce full settlement/liquidation of all outstanding cash advances, otherwise initiate appropriate administrative and/or civil proceeding.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this AAR
No demand letters were sent during the year to employees with outstanding cash advance including those separated from the service. Failure to implement the “No liquidation, No cash advance” policy by the management.
The management has not exhausted all available remedies nor initiate appropriate administrative and/or civil proceedings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: (a) cause the immediate refund of ₱1,380,000.00 SRRI and the additional ₱302,470.00 cash allowance granted to officers and employees; (b) exercise prudence and economy measures in the utilization of government funds; and (c) Submit a written explanation on why the above claim were processed and paid and why the Inspection and Acceptance Report was signed despite the absence of the actual delivery of the purchased items. We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan explain as to the legal basis for appropriating the amount of ₱1,380,000.00 for Special Relief and Recovery Incentive (SRRI) to officers and employees.
observations.reason:
A Notice of Disallowance was issued by the Audit Team.
The management is still not exercising economy measures in the utilization of government funds and has not submitted any written justification on the observation noted.
No explanation was submitted to date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: (a) stop the practice of granting monetary retirement awards to retiring officials and employees; (b) demand the refund of the amount from the retired/separated official/employees who received the monetary retirement award; and (c) strictly conform to the conditions set by CSC in the granting of incentives and awards.
observations.managementAction: No monetary award was granted to retiring officials and employees during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Officer to: (a) strive for the timely and full implementation of LDRRM related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of the fund as well as the desired benefits; (b) ensure that the LDRRMF is specifically utilized for the implementation of projects and activities encompassing the 4 thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management; and (c) strictly observe the provisions of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 in allocating LDRRMF solely for the implementation of DRRM-related programs, project and activities.
observations.reason: Twenty-two PPAs were not implemented during the year and non-formulation of PPAs on disaster rehabilitation and recovery. Moreover, the fund was still utilized for programs that are not related to the key objectives of disaster risk reduction and management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management: (a) stop the lavish spending of the LDRRMF for the repair and maintenance of its transportation equipment particularly for the dry docking of M/L Queen Seagull; (b) ensure that the STF is solely utilized for the activities and projects that support disaster risk reduction and management program; and (c) evaluate the condition of the M/L Queen Seagull by considering cost-benefit analysis to assess whether it needs replacement to avoid incurrence of immoderate and excessive expenditures. Further, the Municipal Accountant (a) submit the pre-repair evaluation report and approved detailed plans by the agency showing in sufficient detail the scope of work/extent of repair to be done; Report of waste materials; Copy of document indicating the history of repair; and Post-inspection reports; and (b) ensure that repairs and maintenance expenditures and its related transactions are supported with the required documents as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 before claims are processed and passed upon for payment.
observations.reason:
Continuous spending on the dry-docking M/L Queen Seagull without considering its condition and cost-benefit analysis.
Pre-repair evaluation report, post-inspection reports, history of repair and other necessary documents required to support the repair expenditures were still not prepared and submitted by the management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee to (a) submit the lacking required documents to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445 for proper/further evaluation of the transactions; and (b) strictly comply with the documentary requirements of Republic Act No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) to establish the eligibility of the suppliers.
observations.reason: The management still failed to comply with the documentary requirements pursuant to RA No. 9184 and its revised IRR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant exert effort to trace the difference and reconcile the respective balances of the Cash in bank balance and unutilized DRRMF transferred to the Special Trust Fund to fully ascertain the correct amount of balances to be reported to present fair presentation of the financial statements at year-end.
observations.reason: The reported balance of Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account is still higher than the Cash in Bank – TF account balance as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU GFPS: (a) strive for the full and effective implementation of GAD-related activities prioritized in the annual GPB, GAD Code, other GAD related policies and plans; and (b) strictly monitor implementation of PPAs to ensure maximum utilization of the fund in order to attain the objective for which the funds were provided that may contribute to local development.
observations.reason:
Planned GAD activities were not fully implemented during the year.
The GPB and the GAD Accomplishment Report were also not submitted to the Auditor on time.

Titling of the land in favor of the Municipality was still not processed by the management.
Complete and accurate property records were not maintained.
Immediate disposal of unserviceable properties is yet to be processed by the management.
The Municipality’s PPE are still not fairly presented in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE: (a) instruct the submission of complete supporting documents on the distribution of the non-food supplies, food packs and drug and medicines; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the supporting documents before processing claims for payment as part of her internal audit services; (c) exercise prudence in disbursing government funds to avoid impractical/unreasonable usage; and (d) cause the necessary postings in the Municipality’s website as part of their public disclosure requirements and to keep it updated. Further, the LCE and all concerned department heads observe strict compliance with the guidelines seth forth under DBM LBC No. 125 dated April 7, 2020 to ensure that the BGCM fund is optimally utilized for necessary expenses to address the adverse effects of the COVID-19 pandemic on the people’s safety, health and livelihood.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office is thriving in ensuring the completeness of the suporting documents before processing payment claims.
observations.reason:
Food and non-food supplies distributed for COVID-19 response were still not duly supported with distribution lists.
Partially implemented in that Municipal Accounting Office is ensuring completeness of supporting documents before processing claims.
The fund was still utilized for payment of rapid antigen testing of employees and residents of with no direct involvement in any activity to address the pandemic situation in the island.
No postings of reports were made during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (a) prepare the necessary reclassification entries on the LDRRM misclassified asset accounts; (b) effect the correcting entries on the disbursements charged to prior years unspent DRRMF in accordance with IPSAS; (c) maintain subsidiary ledgers for various disaster response and rescue equipment in consonance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated; and (d) ensure and observe proper recording and recognition of assets and expenses for the fair presentation of account balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
The municipal accountant has effected the necessary adjusting and correcting entries.
Subsidiary ledgers are now being maintained.
Proper recording has also been observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to: Initiate the needed coordination, monitoring and evaluation on the implementation of all priority development projects within the budget year to ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% DF by proper planning to address the conditions/limitations that hinder the timely implementation of projects; Ensure that projects included in the 20% DF have a long term effect/benefit in the socio economic condition of the constituents, considering the unique environment of the Municipality and include only PPAs which are feasible and applicable to the conditions/limitations in the implementation of 20% DF projects in the island Municipality; Stop the utilization of the fund for expenses not in accordance with guidelines set so that funds could be used to finance more priority development projects. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 and DILG-DBM JMC No. 2020-01 dated March 27, 2020 to ensure that the maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality is achieved.
observations.managementAction:
Majority of the PPAs programed under the fund were started with ongoing implementation.
The management programmed projects which mostly would contribute in the socio economic condition of the constituent.
Management has confirmed that the fund was utilized in accordance with the current provisions and guidelines set.
observations.reason: Twenty-two PPAs were not implemented during the year and some PPAs from current and continuing appropriation were still not implemented during the year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. direct the conduct of physical inventory of the 189 PPE items recorded in the
books, without carrying amount, to determine their existence and condition;
and forward the result to the appraisal committee;
b. create an appraisal committee for the purpose of determining the fair value of
the 189 PPE items to facilitate its recording in the books of accounts;
c. direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher to
recognize the fair value of the 189 PPE items based as determined by the
appraisal committee;
d. direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant and Municipal General Services Officer to
reconcile and update their respective records with respect to the 189 PPE
items; and
e. direct the Municipal General Services Officer to prepare the Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and undertake the
necessary actions in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445 to prevent
further deterioration.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. ICO – Municipal Treasurer to provide the ICO – Municipal Accountant with the
certified list showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and
collectible as basis of the latter in setting-up Real Property Tax Receivables and
Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year for CY 2020;
and
b. ICO – Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to take up the
uncollected receivables by debiting Real Property Tax Receivables/Special
Education Tax Receivables and crediting Deferred Real Property Tax
Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income both by P3,236,343.40 in the
books of General Fund and Special Education Fund, respectively.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the ICO – Municipal
Treasurer to come up with a certified list of taxpayers with uncollected amount of
RPT and SET Receivables from CYs 2002-2019; and submit the same to the ICO –
Municipal Accountant for recording.

Moving forward, instruct the ICO – Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with
Section 20 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant
to make an investigation of the negative balances of liabilities to determine its
causes and effect the necessary adjustments.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO –
Municipal Accountant to disclose the required information in the Notes to
Financial Statements to assist the users in understanding the financial statements
and to provide them with the additional information value.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO – Municipal
Accountant to present the required significant accounting policies in the Notes to
Financial Statements to assist the users to better interpret the financial
statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the Municipal
Engineer to provide the ICO – Municipal Accountant with the complete description
and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the proper
disclosure of the road network system in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant
and the ICO – Municipal Treasurer to:

a. conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the
existence of receivables and determine the concerned debtors according to
paragraph 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005;
b. exert effort in determining the collectability of the receivables, issue demand
letters, and institute all possible remedies to all concerned individuals with
outstanding balances;
c. set up an allowance for impairment for recognition of possible losses
according to paragraphs 67 and 72 of IPSAS 29; and
d. consider requesting authority to write-off receivables with no certainty of
collection from the Commission on Audit, with the reminder for the submission
of all proofs that efforts have been initiated to collect the same;
In addition, require the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer to provide
all possible records relative to the defaulting debtor and coordinate with the ICO –
Municipal Accountant and the ICO – Municipal Treasurer for possible settlement
of the long outstanding receivables.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant
to enhance the presentation of information in the SCF by offering clear distinction
between identified and regular cash inflows such as share from internal revenue
allotment and unique and sporadic cash inflows, as presented in Annex E of COA
Circular No. 2016-004.

Moving forward, direct the ICO – Municipal Accountant to strictly follow the
presentation of SCF in Annex E of COA Circular No. 2016-004.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. ICO – Municipal Accountant to:
• submit immediately the lacking documents for the mentioned disbursements for
afurther verification by the Audit Team;
• refrain from recognizing as outright expense those items classified as inventory,
instead, follow the recording and reporting procedures provided under Section 114
of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. Municipal General Services Officer to prepare RIS and SSMI for proper
monitoring and reporting of the receipts and issuances of inventories and
submit the same to the ICO – Municipal Accountant for the proper amount of
inventory that will be recognized as expense for a period; and
c. Municipal Budget Officer, ICO – Municipal Treasurer, or other Municipal
officials concerned to submit a written justification for the charging of the
inappropriate disbursements totaling P18,516.00 against the BGCM fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all other Municipal officials
concerned to coordinate with the ICO – Municipal Accountant for the submission of the
documents to substantiate the relief operations made by the Municipality and for the
Audit Team to determine whether the procured relief goods were completely distributed
to the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the ICO – Municipal Treasurer
and ICO – Municipal Accountant to remit the barangay’s share from RPT and CTC
collections within five days after the end of each quarter as mandated under Section
271 (d) of LGC and Section 15 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to reduce or eliminate the excess in personal services
and limit expenses within the allowed amount prescribed under RA No. 7160;
b. ICO – Municipal Treasurer to strategize and exert more efforts to increase its
collections to generate additional revenue that would increase the basis in the
computation of the allowable personal services; and
c. Municipal Budget Officer and ICO – Municipal Accountant to explain in writing
why the excess in PS limitation of P7,259,462.79 should not be disallowed in
audit.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal
Budget Officer, ICO – Municipal Treasurer and ICO – Municipal Accountant as well
as the Sanggunian Pambayan to exercise prudence in handling public funds since
they all share fiscal responsibility over financial affairs, transactions and
operation of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the ICO – Municipal Treasurer
to:

a. provide a daily summary of debits and credits in the cashbook;
b. accomplish the required certification monthly to define accountability towards
the transactions recorded in the cashbooks; and
c. fully comply with the guidelines on proper recording of the Municipality’s cash
transactions in the cashbooks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. require the Municipal Engineer and the MPDO to submit to the Audit Team at the
beginning of the year the list infrastructure projects and non-infrastructure
PPAs, respectively, that are on-going and those that are to be implemented
during the year; and
b. inform the Audit Team within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure
project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project
signboards and/or public notices have been already posted in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2013-004.

Moving forward, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all the
concerned persons/departments to submit their action plans and status of
implementation to his Office and consolidate the same using the AAPSI form,
either in printed form or by way of an electronic document for submission to the
Audit Team within 60 days from the receipt of the AAR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to undertake physical inventory of PPE and Inventories, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE, as well as the RPCI to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

Set timetable that will be feasible and applicable to the conditions/limitations in  the implementation of  20% DF projects in the island municipality.
observations.reason: Lack of enough personnel with technical skills to prepare plans and program of works.  Implementation of projects funded by the national government was given more priority.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor address the issue on manpower
constraint and direct the Inventory Committee to undertake physical inventory of
PPE and Inventories, and prepare and submit the RPCPPE, as well as, RPCI to the
Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
▪ Municipal Treasurer to provide the OIC – Municipal Accountant with the
certified list showing the name of taxpayers, and the amount due and
collectible as basis of the latter in setting-up Real Property Tax Receivables and
Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year; and
▪ OIC – Municipal Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to take up the
uncollected receivables by debiting Real Property Tax Receivables/Special
Education Tax Receivables and crediting Deferred Real Property Tax
Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income both by P4,313,135.10 in the
books of General Fund and Special Education Fund, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant to
present the receipt and utilization of funds from National Government Agencies in the
Statement of Cash Flows under the operating activities instead of financing activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – Municipal Accountant
to disclose the required information in the Notes to Financial Statements to assist
the users in understanding the financial statements and to provide them with the
additional information value.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
enjoin the Municipal Engineer to provide the OIC – Municipal Accountant with the
complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to
facilitate the proper disclosure of the road network system in the Notes to Financial
Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
• Municipal Budget Officer to reduce or eliminate the excess in personal services
and limit expenses within the allowed amount prescribed under RA No. 7160;
• Municipal Treasurer to strategize and exert more efforts to increase its
collections in order to generate additional revenue that would increase the
basis in the computation of the allowable personal services; and
• Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to submit documents and
explain in writing why the excess in PS limitation of P14,677,504.67 should not
be disallowed in audit.

For subpart (a):
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
▪ end-users to prepare the purchase request for goods based on the approved
APP; and
▪ BAC Chairperson to explain in writing why the expenditures for the purchase
of goods should not be disallowed in audit.

For subpart (b):
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards
Committee (BAC) and the OIC – Municipal Accountant to:
▪ ensure that the documentary requirements in the procurement of goods
under the methods of Negotiated – Two Failed Biddings and Negotiated –
Emergency Cases are complete before processing for payment; and
▪ submit the lacking documents of expenditures totaling P810,075.00 to avoid
suspension/disallowance in audit.

For subpart (c):
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to:
▪ submit resolutions for the procurement of goods totaling P8,667,114.38
adopting the Shopping/Negotiated – Small Value Procurement; and
▪ require the suppliers to submit the necessary documents in the procurement
of goods under Negotiated – Small Value Procurement such as PhilGEPS
registration, mayor’s/business permit, income/business tax returns and
omnibus sworn statement; and the required documents for Shopping such
as mayor’s/business permit and PhilGEPS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
▪ OIC – Municipal Accountant and the concerned employees to submit to the
Auditor the authority to render overtime, work program, accomplishment
reports and properly accomplished DTR to avoid suspension/disallowance in
audit; and
▪ Municipal Treasurer to ensure that the liquidation report on cash advance for
overtime pay is supported with duly signed payrolls.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Municipal Treasurer to (a) strictly observe the provisions of Section 63 of LTOM in the maintenance of cashbook as it is an important control tool for cash; (b) regularly update the posting/recording in the cashbooks; (c) ensure that transactions are correctly recorded, footed and the balance are brought forward correctly; (d) maintain a separate cashbook per fund for proper monitoring and determination of cash balance for each fund at any given date.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) always check the correctness of reported balance of AF in the RAAF by comparing the ending balances on hand against the actual count of the unused AF under her custody before certifying its correctness; (b) ensure that the Report of Collections and Deposits and the ORs issued are properly filled out and signed; and (c) submit to the Audit Team all the obsolete, spoiled and cancelled ORs and Accountable Forms for inspection and destruction thereof.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor require the inventory team to conduct physical inventory of the breeding stocks/animals taking into consideration their biological transformation; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) maintain the WOABSLC and update the balance of the breeding stocks presented in the FS; and (ii) facilitate the recording of the acquired biological assets amounting to ₱141,606.73 using the correct asset account to present fairly the account balances in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the management (a) cause the immediate refund of the excess amount of PEI totaling ₱1,291,500.00; and (b) henceforth, strictly adhere to the existing laws, rules and regulations on the grant of PEI and other related bonuses and allowances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) recall the positions of  Supervising Tourism Operations Officer and Internal Auditor IV as the creation of these new positions has no legal basis; b) revoke the appointment of Mr. Frisco M. Cervantes it being void from the beginning; c) strictly observe Section 4(d) DBM LBC No. 53 in the creation of new plantilla position as well as CSC Resolution No. 1800010 and other related existing laws, rules and regulations; and d) refund any amount or compensation paid to Mr. Frisco M. Cervantes as Internal Auditor IV pursuant to Section 65, Chapter 10, Book V Executive Order No. 292 and Section 4, Rule VI Revised Omnibus Rules on Appointments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Revise the authority given to the Municipal Administrator limiting his approving authority to those expenditures/claims involving regularly recurring administrative expenses pursuant to Section 344 of R.A. 7160;
Appoint a full-time Budget Officer it being a mandatory position for the municipality under Section 475 of R.A. No. 7160; and
Install, implement, and monitor a sound system of internal control pursuant to Sections 123 and 124 of PD 1445 to ensure existence of effective check and balance to safeguard the assets and resources of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management (a) facilitate the return of unexpended balances of completed projects totaling ₱1,698,975.65 or request from the source agencies to re-program the unexpended funds to other related projects; and (b)  expedite the implementation of four trust fund projects costing ₱16,624,000.00 to provide the expected benefits to the intended beneficiaries on time and forestall the possible cancellation thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer cause the actual cash transfer of the 2019 unutilized QRF and DRRM-MOOE amounting to ₱723,383.59 from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund Account.
We recommended that the LCE instruct the MDRRM Council to incorporate in the LDRRMF the PPAs to be funded by the previous year’s unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality in building a resilient community.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LDRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month as required under COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper monitoring and facilitate its submission to the oversight bodies.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRM Officer to (a) prepare and maintain Stock Cards and Property Cards of all procured items in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002; (b) trace the persons who are in actual possession and accountable for equipment that are still under the name of the MDDRM Officer and issue the corresponding ARE therefor; and (c) prepare RIS for emergency goods distributed/issued to the beneficiaries and  promptly submit the same to the accounting office to facilitate proper and timely reporting, and thus ensure fair presentation of inventories and expense account balances in the financial statements.

Notes to Financial Statements.

prepare the accounting entries shown below to establish Other Receivables in

direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately provide and maintain complete
subsidiary ledgers for the special accounts in compliance with Section 313 of

protect its interest from third party claims and illegal dispositions.

Receivable based on the certified list of taxpayers provided by the

Property Officer maintain complete property cards for all PPE with complete information and ensure that all government equipment are supported by PAR upon issuance to end users;
Municipal Accountant investigate the unreconciled difference of ₱5,442,803.50. Record the municipal ambulance in the books and also compute the corresponding depreciation. Henceforth, all donated items received by the Municipality should be recorded using the appropriate asset account to present fairly the account balances in the Financial Statements; and
Since the land could not be subject for titling, the Municipal Mayor inquire from the DENR remedies to secure and take actual possession of the land.   It is his responsibility to ensure that all real estate properties are registered under the Torrens Title System and safeguarded from squatters and unlawful occupants.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) render written explanation and valid justification why they resorted to negotiated procurement instead of public bidding even without complying the provision of Section 35.5 of RA No. 9184; (b) require the captain of the vessel to submit Master Declaration of Safe Departure (MDSD), Crew List, Passenger Manifest, Coasting Manifest, Cargo Manifest, Certificate of Orderly Inspection (COI) for the February 11, March 11 and May 5, 2018 trips; and (c) require Hja. Roselyn I. Jain, owner of the vessel, to explain why Mr. Nolito P. Verdeflor was listed as the captain of M/L Alkhaen-3 and issue corresponding ORs for the payments of the services of chartered vessel.
Henceforth, the management refrain from procuring services without the benefit of competitive bidding and observe strict compliance with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR on the procurement of goods and services.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer (a) evaluate the condition of the watercrafts by considering cost-benefit analysis to identify which among them are still beneficial to the municipality and which needs replacement to avoid incurrence of immoderate and higher expenditures; (b) evaluate the skills and capabilities of their employed personnel if they are qualified enough to perform repair of watercrafts; and (c) maintain a record of trips of all of its transportation equipment, specifically the watercrafts to justify the number and extent of repairs done.
Further, the Municipal Accountant (a) maintain record of repairs for each government equipment indicating therein the type of repair undertaken, the date when it was made and other pertinent data as required in Circular No. 81-131A; and (b) ensure that repairs and maintenance expenditures and its related transactions are supported by the required documents as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-001 before claims are processed and passed upon for payment.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor exercise prudence in the utilization of government funds; and (b) PRAISE Committee carefully evaluate the validity and basis of giving incentives and whether those granted are qualified to receive the same.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate refund of ₱500,000.00 as cash incentives through PRAISE program granted to officers and employees.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the disbursement and liquidation vouchers, as part of the internal audit system of the municipal government to determine completeness and regularity of supporting documents and ensure compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (b) Municipal Mayor instruct all concerned employees to submit the noted lacking documents.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Muncipal Planning and Development Coordinator to:
Maximize the utilization of the 20% DF through prompt  implementation of  projects within the budget year by finding ways to address the conditions/limitations in the delay of implementation of projects as pointed out by the Audit Team;
Ensure that projects included in the 20% DF have a long term effect/benefit in the socio economic condition  of the constituents, considering the unique environment of the municipality and ensure that PPAs under the 20%DF are among the allowable development projects as enumerated in JMC No. 2017-01; and
Set timetable that will be feasible and applicable to the conditions/limitations in  the implementation of  20% DF projects in the island municipality.

We recommended that the local Chief Executive fast track the implementation  of Trust Fund projects to ensure that socio economic benefits expected from these projects will be enjoyed by the constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Property Officer to (a) prepare and maintain an inventory list of all procured and donated items based on the updated Stock Cards and Property Cards in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002; (b) maintain RIS of goods held for emergency distribution; and (c) ensure that all equipment purchased out of DRRMF issued to end-users are supported by PAR.
Further, the MDRRM Officer (a) coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the correct DRRM equipment to be accounted for under the Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment; and (b) ensure that the List of Inventories of Relief Goods and MDRRM equipment to be submitted are in accordance with the prescribed formats provided under the same Circular.

We recommended that the management hasten the approval of the Solid Waste Management Plan by immediately integrating the revised WACS as required by the PCSD and for them to implement Solid Waste Management programs and projects that would improve the solid waste facilities and ensure the protection of public health of the constituents of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipality’s GAD Focal Point System lead in  monitoring the effective implementation of the annual GPB, GAD Code, other GAD related policies and plans and ensure that GAD fund is optimally utilized for its intended purposes and regularly monitor the actual cost of implementing the GAD activities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to strictly adhere to Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of E.O. No. 292 on the guidelines on Monetization of Leave Credits. 

We recommended the HRMO should exercise prudence in recommending approval  for the grant of leave monetization. Ensure that official and employees availing for monetization of vacation leave credits has accumulated no less than 15 days, in which case he can monetize no more than ten days and that five days of VL credits will remain after monetization which shall be availed only once a year. Stop allowing the practice of monetizing both the vacation and sick leave credit balances. In granting special monetization, strictly follow the conditions that the monetization applied for was 50% or more of the accumulated sick leave balances.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to check the validity and completeness of the required supporting documents as stated in the Item 5.14 of COA Cirular No. 2012-001 before processing any payments for leave monetization.

The LDRRMO strictly utilize the LDRRMF solely for disaster risk reduction and management activities pursuant to R.A. No. 10121 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012. For this purpose, unnecessary expenditures should be avoided to ensure the availability of resources in mitigating the effects of calamities.

The LDRRMO should likewise prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

The Municipal Treasurer to open and maintain a separate depository bank account for the 5% Calamity Fund and transfer all the prior years’ unexpended balances to ensure that funds are properly monitored and secured from possible misuse and misappropriation and that the funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council in times of calamities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect the necessary
reclassification entries and observe proper recording of the transactions in
consonance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 in order to present fairly the
accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to make an effort to (i)
identify the long outstanding/ dormant cash advances; and (ii) serve written
reminders/notices to officials and employees with outstanding cash advances and
require them to settle their obligations immediately by submitting liquidation
reports with complete supporting documents and/or refund the balance of
unused cash advance/s;
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to file request for authority
to write-off dormant cash advances as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2016-005
dated December 19, 2016 as these affect the fair presentation of the financial
statements.

We recommended the Management that the Municipal Accountant submit
the vouchers, supporting documents, utilization reports, contracts and other
supporting documents to COA in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013 and
DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-03 in all fund transfers.

a. The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the foregoing items to the appropriate
inventory accounts and record the corresponding expenses upon issuance of
the same.
b. The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to prepare and
maintain an inventory list of all procured items. Issue an RIS upon
distribution to end users/beneficiaries.
c. The Local Chief Executive to ensure that all the procured supplies are
safeguarded against loss and wastage through illegal or improper disposition.
All of the MDRRM transactions should be properly monitored in accordance
with the existing rules and regulations on the utilization of Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Fund.

We recommended the Management to strictly adhere to Rule XVI of the
Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of E.O. No. 292 on the guidelines on
Monetization of Leave Credits.
We recommended the HRMO should exercise prudence in recommending
approval for the grant of leave monetization. Ensure that official and employees
availing for monetization of vacation leave credits has accumulated no less than
15 days, in which case he can monetize no more than ten days and that five days
of VL credits will remain after monetization which shall be availed only once a
year. Stop allowing the practice of monetizing both the vacation and sick leave
credit balances. In granting special monetization, strictly follow the conditions
that the monetization applied for was 50% or more of the accumulated sick leave
balances.
We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to check the validity and
completeness of the required supporting documents as stated in the Item 5.14 of
COA Cirular No. 2012-001 before processing any payments for leave
monetization.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Engineer (ME) and Municipal Planning and Development
Coordinator (MPDC) reevaluate and complete the list of infrastructure
projects in the monitoring report;
b. MPDC and Local Development Council include in the Annual Investment
Plan only those programs and projects which are feasible, specific and
properly identified as to target date of execution and the official in-charge
responsible for the implementation; and
c. MPDC coordinate with the ME to ensure that infrastructure projects are
implemented timely and efficiently.

We recommended that the Management hasten the approval of the Solid
Waste Management Plan by immediately acting upon on any
comments/recommendations from the PCSD and to allocate budget for the
implementation of the SWMP to improve the solid waste facilities and ensure the
protection of public health of the constituents of the municipality.

We recommended that the BAC prepare the PMR in its prescribed format
after the end of each semester for the approval and submission by the Municipal
Mayor to the GPPB, in compliance with Section 12.2 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the LDRRMF unexpended
balance for CY 2017 and years thereafter to the Special Trust Fund pursuant
to Section 21 of RA No. 10121.
b. The LDRRMO strictly utilize the LDRRMF solely for disaster risk reduction
and management activities pursuant to R.A. No. 10121 and DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012. For this purpose,
unnecessary expenditures should be avoided to ensure the availability of
resources in mitigating the effects of calamities.
c. The LDRRMO should likewise prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2012-002.
d. The Municipal Treasurer to open and maintain a separate depository bank
account for the 5% Calamity Fund and transfer all the prior years’
unexpended balances to ensure that funds are properly monitored and secured
from possible misuse and misappropriation and that the funds are available for
disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Local Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Council in times of calamities.

We recommended that the BAC and the BAC Secretariat strictly abide by the
posting requirement in the PhilGEPS of all procurement opportunities, notices,
awards and other related information, in compliance with Sections 8.2.1(a), 37.1.6
and 37.4.2 of the 2016 Revised I.R.R. of R.A. No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Department Heads
concerned to prepare a consolidated report on all management actions taken to
implement the audit receommendations provided on the Annual Audit Rerport for
CY 2016 using the prescribed AAPSI form and submit the same to the Audit Team.
Likewise furnish the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), the
House Committee on Appropriations and the Senate Committee on Finance, either
in printed form or by way of electronic document, a copy of said report.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to (a) examine the cause of the discrepancy; (b) reconcile the reciprocal accounts; and (c) effect adjustments to reflect the correct amount of the accounts for the fair presentation of the financial statements.
We recommended that the management stop the practice of granting additional incentive benefits, whether in cash or in kind, to the municipal officers and employees without legal basis.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and the updated guidelines on cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local School Board Officials to strictly utilize the SEF for purposes provided under Section 1 of RA No. 5447, Sections 272 and 100 of RA No. 7160 and Joint Circular No. 01 of DECS-DBM-DILG, s. of 1998.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise all the concerned officials to refund the amount of ₱13,100.00 (₱2,500 plus ₱10,600) as payment of membership fees and annual dues to private association/organization and strictly observe the provisions of RA No. 7160 and NBC No. 563 dated April 22, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit the RCI and ensure that all cancelled checks are attached therein upon submission to the Municipal Accountant, in compliance with Sections 55 and 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volumes I and II, respectively.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that:
	the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant the Monthly RCI and RCD within five days after the end of the month to allow prompt recording of the transactions in the books of account, pursuant to Section 6.03 of COA Circular No. 95-006.
	the Municipal Accountant turn over the receipts and disbursement documents evidencing the transactions to the Office of the Auditor within ten days from the date of receipt of said documents, pursuant to Section 6.04 of the same COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MGSO and BAC Chairman to strictly adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 87-278, as restated and updated under COA Circular No. 2009-001, for the timely review of contracts and POs and conduct of inspection of deliveries and infrastructure projects.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to (a) examine the cause of
the discrepancy; (b) reconcile the reciprocal accounts; and (c) effect adjustments
to reflect the correct amount of the accounts for the fair presentation of the
financial statements.

We recommended the Management to impose all available actions as
provided by laws and regulations on the settlement of cash advances. Make an
effort to identify the long outstanding/ dormant cash advances and request for
write-off as these affect the fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant to intensify the monitoring
of cash advances and ensure timely submission of Report of Unliquidated Cash
Advances to the Audit Team. For dormant cash advances, facilitate the filing of
request for write-off with complete supporting documents in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the management render explanation or valid
justification on the above noted observations and deficiencies. We also
recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC members and BAC
Secretariat to immediately submit the above lacking documents to avoid audit
suspension/disallowance. Henceforth, we recommended strict compliance with
the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, R.A. No. 9184
and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the immediate
submission of the lacking documents. Henceforth, we recommended strict
compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14,
2012, R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer (a) submit the complete
detailed engineering documents, SWA, pictures and other relevant documents of
the implemented projects and strictly comply with Annex A of Revised I.R.R. of
R.A. No. 9184; and (b) supervise and validate the status of implementation/
construction of all infrastructure projects implemented by the Municipality of
Kalayaan, whether implemented thru straight contract or by administration,
pursuant to the duties and functions of the City Engineer provided in Section
477.b of R.A. No. 7160.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the
approved program of works, duly accomplished SWA signed/approved by the
Municipal Engineer, pictures of the project and other relevant documents that
will substantiate the payment for the construction materials and labor
component of the project.

We recommended that the management stop the practice of granting
additional incentive benefits, whether in cash or in kind, to municipal officers
and employees without legal basis.

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to transfer the CY 2016 LDRRMF
unexpended balance of ₱ 1,376,722.18 to the Special Trust Fund pursuant to
Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

b. The LDRRMO strictly utilize the LDRRMF solely for disaster risk reduction
and management activities pursuant to R.A. No. 10121 and DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012. For this purpose,
unnecessary expenditures should be avoided to ensure the availability of
resources in mitigating the effects of calamities.

c. The LDRRMO should likewise prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2012-002.

d. The Municipal Mayor to appoint for the support staff plantilla position in
compliance with Section 12 of RA 10121.

e. The Municipal Treasurer to open and maintain a separate depository bank
account for the 5% Calamity Fund and transfer all the prior years’ unexpended
balances to ensure that funds are properly monitored and secured from possible
misuse and misappropriation and that the funds are available for disaster risk
reduction and management activities of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction
Management Council in times of calamities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the submission of all
the required supporting documents for the procurement of one unit brand new
motor vehicle. In all instances, the Municipal Mayor must ensure strict
adherence to the provisions of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated
March 25, 2013, DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2014-155 dated December
17, 2014 and other guidelines and issuances related to the proper utilization of
the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund.

We recommended the following:

a. Municipal Mayor to maximize the utilization of 20% Development Fund to
include projects which are feasible, specific and properly identified as to
target date of execution and the Municipal Engineering personnel in-charge
responsible for the effective implementation.

b. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator together with the
Local Development Council to coordinate, monitor and evaluate the
implementation of the different development programs, projects and
activities in accordance with the approved development plan and shall
render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems
encountered and recommend measures to address such problem and submit
year end accomplishment report stating the completed, on-going and
awarded projects with remarks to the Audit Team on the prescribed period.

We recommended the LCPC to submit the items enumerated in Part II of
DILG MC No. 2012-120. We also recommended the Municipal Budget Officer to
submit a detailed budget for the LCPC supported by the appropriation
ordinance from the Sangguniang Bayan. Henceforth, the management should
abide to the rules and regulations with regards to the formulation of budget and
utilization of the 1% LCPC fund.

We recommended strict adherence to the guidelines for the preparation
of annual GAD plans and budgets and accomplishment reports provided in
PCW, NEDA and DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01 to ensure that the programs
that will be implemented under GAD are directed to improving the municipal
government’s capacity for gender mainstreaming, addressing gender issues
and/or promoting gender equality.

We recommended that Management facilitate the preparation of the 10-
year Solid Waste Management Plan. Likewise, the Management should comply
with all applicable rules and regulations of Ecological Solid Waste Management
Act of 2000 to improve the solid waste facilities and ensure the protection of
public health of its constituents.
